input_statement: We need to minimize the use of Visual C++ Runtime.
The malloc, memcpy, str* functions should be avoided.
WinAPI already has everything you need - instead of malloc - HeapAlloc, instead of memcpy - CopyMemory, instead of strstr - StrStr (shlwapi.h/.lib).
1.1 STL should be avoided in general.
C: in general

input_statement: STL does not work with std::mutex.
The rest is at your own risk!
Some STL chips give additional prologue to the code.
For std::mutex it weighs 200k.
It is not visible in your code, but it is there and will behave strangely in the context of execution!
1.3 Not following points 1 and 1.1 can cause VERY strange behavior.

input_statement: Ignoring or not knowing these points is usually the reason why you have a module running locally and not in production.
Also, there are additional requirements related to security:
Temporary files are prohibited.
By default, writing to any files and registry is prohibited.
Each case of such writing must be specified.
If possible, the module should not have a state (i.e., it should not need to save anything to the registry/file when stopped and then read from it when started).
If the module runs in multiple processes, the best way to communicate between them (in descending order - remember that temporary files are forbidden):

input_statement: The pseudo-code is very roughly like this:
main() {
LoadLibrary("module.dll");
Start = GetProcAddress(module, "Start");
Handle = Start(...);
If(!handle) return 1;
Control("config", "config body");
While(true) sleep(1000);
Release(handle);
Thus, the following scheme:
1. demo launcher loads a module (DLL) to itself and calls start and control functions to pass configs, external ip address, clientid and group to it.
2. code inside the module that does useful work.
The execution flow is as follows:
* The Start() function initializes the job, starts the main worker thread in the background, and returns a module descriptor (usually the address of the Start function)
C: starts the main worker thread in the background, and returns a module descriptor

input_statement: * The main thread can wait until the necessary Control() calls with configs arrive and only then start working.
C: until they arrive

input_statement: It depends on the purpose of the module itself - there may be no need to wait.
C: the purpose of the module itself

input_statement: * Control() calls are usually used to pass configs or control actions (change of operating modes) to the module.
C: to the module, change of operating modes

input_statement: These calls must be handled as interrupts.
These calls take place in a separate thread, so thread safety should be ensured for everything,
C: for everything

input_statement: that is shared with the main thread of execution.
MODULE API
The module exports the following functions: Start, Control, Release, FreeBuffer [*1]
All functions have the calling convention stdcall and are exported by name.
B: calling convention stdcall and are exported by name

input_statement: The Start function has the following prototype:
PVOID Start(
LPCSTR ModuleName,
LPCBYTE Arg,
SIZE_T ArgLen,
LPSTR ResultInfo,
const ParentInfo* pParentData,
PVOID EventCallback,
PVOID EventCallbackContext,
PVOID Reserved1);
The function is called when the module is started by the above logic.
C: when the module is started by the above logic

input_statement: ModuleName - module name
Arg - argument of the start command
ArgLen - size of CtlArg parameter in bytes
ResultInfo - buffer for the resulting ctl string.
The buffer has a fixed length of 1024 bytes.
C: fixed length of 1024 bytes

input_statement: pParentData - information about the above logic, controlled by the module's config.
EventCallback - pointer to the logging function (see below)
EventCallbackContext - logging context (see below)
In case of success, the function returns a descriptor to be used when calling Control and Release functions (you can pass the address of the Start function itself - it is quite unique within the OS).
C: in case of success, when calling Control and Release functions

input_statement: In case of failure, the function returns null.
C: in case of failure

input_statement: typedef struct ParentInfo {
CHAR ParentID[256];
CHAR ParentGroup[64]; CHAR SelfIP[64]
CHAR SelfIP[64];
LPCWSTR ParentFiles;
ParentID - the full client ID of the above logic
ParentGroup - grouping tag of the above logic
SelfIP - external IP address
ParentFiles - not used
6.2 The Control function has the following prototype
BOOL Control (
PVOID ModuleHandle,
LPCSTR Ctl,
LPCBYTE CtlArg,
SIZE_T CtlArgLen,
LPSTR CtlResultInfo,
PVOID*
ppOutData,
PDWORD pOutDataSize,
LPCSTR pOutDataTag,
PVOID Reserved1);
ModuleHandle - module descriptor returned by the Start function.
Ctl - string containing the description of the control signal to the module
CtlArg - ctl argument to the module (signal body)
CtlArgLen - size of the CtlArg parameter in bytes
ResultInfo - buffer for the resulting ctl string.
The buffer has a fixed length of 1024 bytes.
C: fixed length of 1024 bytes

input_statement: ppOutData - pointer to the variable where the pointer to the buffer with ctl output data (ctl_OutData) will be saved.
pOutDataSize - pointer to the variable into which the data size in the buffer with output data will be saved ctl
pOutDataTag - buffer for the auxiliary tag that will be sent to the server.
The buffer has a fixed length of 128 bytes
The function returns TRUE in case of success, otherwise the function returns FALSE.
C: in case of success/otherwise

input_statement: In case of success, if the value of *ppOutData after the call is not equal to zero, this buffer must be freed via the FreeBuffer function.
C: via the FreeBuffer function, if the value of *ppOutData after the call is not equal to zero, in case of success

input_statement: The usual purpose of this function is to pass the configuration to the module.
C: to the module

input_statement: The Release function has the following prototype
VOID Release (
PVOID ModuleHandle);
The function implements the complete termination of the module's operation.
B: complete termination of the module's operation

input_statement: Its tasks include deleting all resources used during the module operation.
B: all resources used during the module operation

input_statement: The FreeBuffer function has the following prototype
VOID FreeBuffer (
PVOID pMemory);
The function frees the buffer allocated inside the Control function (parameter ppOutData).
C: allocated inside the Control function (parameter ppOutData)

Functions can be changed during cleanup, but again you will need to inform the administrator.
C: during cleanup

EXPORTS

@2 = Control_
@3 = FreeBuffer_

Example def file for export by name:

Reload = FreeBuffer_

RULES FOR LOGGING EVENTS TO THE ADMIN
The penultimate two parameters of the Start function are for logging events to the admin area.
C: the penultimate two parameters of the Start function

input_statement: EventCallback is a pointer to a function defined as:
typedef VOID (__stdcall *pEventCallback)(
PVOID ModuleHandle,
LPCSTR EventName,
LPCSTR EventInfo,
PVOID pOutData,
DWORD OutDataSize,
LPCSTR pOutDataTag,
PVOID Context);
where:
ModuleHandle - module descriptor (usually a pointer to the Start function)
EventName - event type
EventInfo - event content
pOutData - additional event data
OutDataSize - length of additional data
pOutDataTag - event tag
Context - pass here the EventCallbackContext received in Start
Define a function like this in your code
void SendEvent(char* name, char* value) {
pEventCallback callback = (pEventCallback)EvCallback;
if (callback) {
debug_printf("SendEvent(%s, %s)\r\n", name, value);
callback(Start, name, value, NULL, 0, tag, EvCallbackContext);
where
tag - event tag (needed for filtering in logs; it is usually the name of a module, e.g. "module1")
name - event source (many events can be grouped by their source - the subsystem in the code where they are generated.
For example, "sql")
value - event content (an arbitrary string.
For example, "module1 build 01 Jan, 20xx 11:22:25 started ok")
The developer defines the source of the event himself.
The source must consist of 4 characters.
For example, you can define types DEBG for debugging, VERS for sending your own version, PING for sending heartbeat to the admin, etc.
C: for example, you can define types DEBG for debugging, VERS for sending your own version, PING for sending heartbeat to the admin, etc.

input_statement: A source that identifies the subsystem of your module from which the event originates.
For example, a module has a file subsystem and a network subsystem - the tags "file" and "net" are used for them.
C: for example, a module has a file subsystem and a network subsystem
